description: When a consumer fetches a data_refs[].location over HTTPS, an HTTP redirect to the SAME host but a DIFFERENT port MUST be refused. Per the same-authority definition (RFC-ACDP-0006 §7.5, cross-referenced from RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.9), two URLs share an authority only when scheme, host, and effective port all match. A redirect from https://data.example.com/file to https://data.example.com:8443/file changes the effective port and therefore the service boundary; a host-only comparison would wrongly follow it. This is a consumer fetch-time check: the body itself stays valid.
ssrf_rule: RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.9 (DataRef location SSRF protection — same-authority = scheme + host + effective port, RFC-ACDP-0006 §7.5)

input.scenario: Consumer fetches https://data.example.com/file; the server returns a 30x whose Location header keeps the host but changes the port

additional_test_cases.note: explicit :443 is the SAME effective port as the https default — same authority, redirect MAY be followed

expected.behavior: The consumer MUST refuse the cross-port redirect. The authority comparison MUST be over scheme + host (case-insensitive) + effective port (the port after applying the scheme default — 443 for https). A host-only comparison is non-conformant. The DataRef MUST be treated as unfetchable and unverifiable.
expected.non_conformant_implementation: A redirect check that compares only the hostname and treats a :8443 redirect target as same-authority with the :443 origin.
expected.rationale: A different port is a different service boundary. An attacker who controls data.example.com on port 443 may not control an internal service the host also exposes on 8443; conflating the two re-opens the SSRF path the redirect cap is meant to close.
